name: Barbell Back Squat
instructions: Position the barbell on a squat rack at chest height. Step under the bar and rest it on your upper trapeziuses, not on your neck.
Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width. Unrack by extending your legs and step back two paces.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointed slightly outward (15-30 degrees).
Brace your core, take a deep breath, and descend by pushing your hips back and bending your knees simultaneously.
Lower until your thighs are parallel to the floor or slightly below, keeping your chest up and knees tracking over your toes.
Drive through your heels to return to the starting position. Do not lock out your knees at the top.

name: Front Squat
instructions: Set the barbell on a rack at shoulder height. Cross your arms and place the bar on your front deltoids, or use a clean grip with fingers under the bar and elbows high.
Step back and position your feet slightly wider than hip-width, toes slightly out.
Keep your elbows high throughout the movement to prevent the bar from rolling forward.
Descend by pushing your knees out and dropping your hips straight down, keeping your torso as upright as possible.
Go to at least parallel, then drive through the floor to stand back up.

name: Conventional Deadlift
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, bar over mid-foot. Grip the bar just outside your legs with a double-overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips to lower yourself to the bar. Your shins should be nearly vertical and close to the bar.
Take a deep breath, brace your core hard, and pull your chest up to create a neutral spine.
Push the floor away from you rather than thinking about pulling the bar up. Keep the bar close to your body throughout.
Lock out hips and knees simultaneously at the top. Do not hyperextend your lower back.
Lower the bar in a controlled manner by pushing your hips back first, then bending the knees once the bar passes them.

name: Romanian Deadlift
instructions: Stand holding a barbell at hip level with an overhand grip, feet hip-width apart.
Keep a slight bend in your knees throughout — this is not a squat, it is a hip hinge.
Push your hips back and lower the bar along your legs, keeping it close to your body.
Lower until you feel a deep stretch in your hamstrings, typically when the bar reaches mid-shin. Keep your spine neutral.
Drive your hips forward to return to the starting position, squeezing your glutes at the top.

name: Barbell Lunge
instructions: Place a barbell across your upper back as you would for a squat. Stand with feet hip-width apart.
Take a large step forward with one leg. Lower your back knee toward the floor while keeping your torso upright.
Your front knee should stay over your front foot, not caving inward.
Drive through your front heel to return to the starting position, then alternate legs.
Keep your core braced throughout to protect your lower back.

name: Leg Press
instructions: Sit in the leg press machine with your back flat against the pad. Place your feet shoulder-width apart on the platform.
Release the safety handles. Lower the platform toward your chest by bending your knees to approximately 90 degrees.
Do not let your lower back round off the seat as you lower the weight.
Press the platform away from you by extending your legs, stopping just short of locking out your knees.
Return the platform under control. Re-engage the safety handles when finished.

name: Lying Leg Curl
instructions: Lie face down on the leg curl machine with the pad resting just above your heels.
Grip the handles for stability. Your hips should remain flat on the bench throughout.
Curl your heels toward your glutes as far as possible without lifting your hips.
Hold the contraction for one second at the top, then lower slowly back to the starting position.
Do not swing or use momentum — control the weight on both the concentric and eccentric phases.

name: Leg Extension
instructions: Sit in the leg extension machine with the pad resting on the top of your ankles. Adjust the seat so your knees align with the machine's pivot point.
Grip the handles for stability and keep your back against the pad.
Extend your legs until they are fully straight, squeezing your quads hard at the top.
Lower the weight slowly back to the starting position without letting the weight stack touch down.

name: Barbell Bench Press
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with your eyes under the bar. Plant your feet flat on the floor.
Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width. Unrack the bar and position it over your upper chest.
Retract your shoulder blades and maintain a slight arch in your lower back for stability.
Lower the bar in a controlled arc to your mid-chest, keeping your elbows at about a 45-degree angle from your torso.
Press the bar back up explosively to the starting position. Do not bounce the bar off your chest.
Re-rack the bar under control when your set is complete.

name: Incline Barbell Bench Press
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45 degree incline. Lie back with your eyes under the bar.
Grip slightly wider than shoulder-width. Unrack and position the bar over your upper chest.
Lower the bar to your upper chest, just below your collar bones, in a controlled arc.
Press back up explosively, keeping your shoulder blades retracted throughout.
The incline angle shifts emphasis to the upper portion of the pectoralis major and anterior deltoid.

name: Overhead Press
instructions: Set the bar at clavicle height in a rack. Grip the bar just outside shoulder-width with a full grip (thumbs around the bar).
Take the bar out of the rack and step back. Stand with feet hip-width apart, glutes and core tight.
Press the bar directly overhead, moving your head slightly back to allow the bar to pass, then forward once it clears your face.
Finish with the bar over the center of your feet, arms fully extended and ears between your biceps.
Lower the bar back to the front of your shoulders under control.

name: Push-Up
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, arms fully extended.
Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels. Do not let your hips sag or pike up.
Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows, keeping them at about a 45-degree angle from your torso.
When your chest nearly touches the floor, press yourself back up to the starting position.
Exhale as you push up, inhale as you lower down.

name: Dips
instructions: Grip the parallel bars with arms fully extended, body hanging vertically.
Lean slightly forward to engage the chest more, or stay upright to emphasize the triceps.
Lower your body by bending your elbows until your upper arms are parallel to the floor.
Press yourself back up to the starting position without locking out your elbows aggressively.
Avoid swinging or using momentum. Add weight with a dipping belt as you get stronger.

name: Dumbbell Bench Press
instructions: Lie on a flat bench holding a dumbbell in each hand at chest level, palms facing forward.
Press both dumbbells upward simultaneously until your arms are nearly fully extended.
Lower them slowly in a controlled arc back to the starting position, feeling a stretch in your chest.
Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion than a barbell and help correct strength imbalances between sides.

name: Pull-Up
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder-width, arms fully extended.
Depress your shoulder blades (pull them down and together) before pulling.
Pull your chest toward the bar by driving your elbows down and back.
Aim to get your chin over the bar at the top.
Lower yourself under full control until your arms are straight again. Avoid swinging.

name: Close-Grip Pull-Up
instructions: Hang from a bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart.
Keep your core tight and body straight. Initiate the movement by depressing your shoulder blades.
Pull your chest toward the bar, driving your elbows down close to your sides.
The supinated grip places more emphasis on the biceps compared to a standard pull-up.
Lower yourself slowly to a full hang before the next repetition.

name: Barbell Bent-Over Row
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a barbell with an overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Hinge at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor. Maintain a neutral spine.
Let the bar hang at arm's length, then pull it toward your lower abdomen by driving your elbows up and back.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top of the movement.
Lower the bar under control to the starting position. Avoid rounding your lower back.

name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Row
instructions: Place your right knee and right hand on a flat bench for support. Hold a dumbbell in your left hand.
Keep your back flat and parallel to the floor. Let the dumbbell hang at arm's length.
Pull the dumbbell toward your hip, driving your elbow up toward the ceiling.
Squeeze your lat at the top, then lower the dumbbell slowly back to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one side before switching.

name: Cable Lat Pulldown
instructions: Sit at the lat pulldown machine and secure your knees under the pad. Grip the bar wider than shoulder-width with an overhand grip.
Lean back slightly (about 10-15 degrees) and depress your shoulder blades.
Pull the bar down to your upper chest by driving your elbows toward your hips.
Hold the contraction briefly, then let the bar rise slowly under control to full arm extension.
Avoid pulling the bar behind your neck — this can strain the cervical spine.

name: Seated Cable Row
instructions: Sit at the cable row station with your feet on the platform. Grip a close-grip handle with both hands.
Keep your back straight and torso upright — do not lean back excessively.
Pull the handle toward your lower abdomen by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at full contraction, then extend your arms slowly to return the weight.
Maintain tension in your core throughout to protect your lower back.

name: Barbell Curl
instructions: Stand holding a barbell with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart, arms fully extended at your sides.
Keep your elbows close to your torso and fixed throughout the movement — they should not drift forward.
Curl the barbell upward by contracting your biceps until the bar reaches shoulder level.
Squeeze your biceps hard at the top, then lower the bar slowly back to the starting position.
Do not use your back or momentum to complete the lift — control the weight on both phases.

name: Hammer Curl
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing each other), arms at your sides.
Keep your elbows fixed at your sides. Curl one or both dumbbells upward without rotating your wrist.
The neutral grip targets the brachialis and brachioradialis more than a standard curl.
Lower the weight slowly and under control. Alternate arms or perform both simultaneously.

name: Cable Tricep Pushdown
instructions: Attach a straight bar or rope to the high cable pulley. Stand facing the machine and grip the attachment with an overhand grip.
Keep your elbows close to your sides and fixed — they should not flare out or move forward.
Push the bar down until your arms are fully extended. Squeeze your triceps hard at the bottom.
Return the bar slowly to the starting position, allowing your forearms to rise to about parallel with the floor.
Use the rope attachment to increase range of motion and independent arm movement.

name: EZ-Bar Skull Crusher
instructions: Lie on a flat bench and hold an EZ-bar with a close, slightly angled grip, arms extended over your chest.
Lower the bar toward your forehead by bending only at your elbows. Keep your upper arms perpendicular to the floor.
When the bar is near your forehead, press it back to the starting position by extending your elbows.
Do not let your elbows flare out to the sides — keep them pointing toward the ceiling throughout.
A spotter is recommended when using heavy loads.

name: Dumbbell Lateral Raise
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides, palms facing inward.
Keeping a slight bend in your elbows, raise both dumbbells out to the sides until they reach shoulder height.
Lead with your elbows, not your wrists. Tilt the front of the dumbbell slightly down (like pouring water) to maximize medial deltoid activation.
Lower the dumbbells under control to the starting position. Do not use momentum or shrug your traps.

name: Dumbbell Front Raise
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand in front of your thighs, palms facing your thighs.
Raise one or both dumbbells directly in front of you to shoulder height, keeping your elbows slightly bent.
Pause briefly at shoulder height, then lower the weight slowly to the starting position.
Avoid swinging your torso or using momentum. Alternate arms to increase time under tension.

name: Cable Rear Delt Fly
instructions: Set both cables to the highest position on a cable crossover machine. Stand in the center and grab the left cable with your right hand and the right cable with your left hand (cross them).
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, slight bend in elbows.
Pull both cables outward and back in a wide arc, squeezing your rear deltoids and upper back.
Return to the starting position under control.
Alternatively, perform this exercise bent over at 90 degrees with cables set at floor level.

name: Plank
instructions: Place your forearms on the floor with elbows directly below your shoulders. Extend your legs behind you, resting on your toes.
Keep your body in a perfectly straight line from head to heels. Do not let your hips sag or rise.
Brace your core as if you were about to take a punch. Squeeze your glutes and quads.
Breathe steadily throughout. Hold the position for the prescribed time.
If your form breaks, stop the set — a short plank with good form is better than a long one with poor form.

name: Ab Wheel Rollout
instructions: Kneel on the floor and grip the ab wheel with both hands, arms extended directly below your shoulders.
Brace your core hard and slowly roll the wheel forward, extending your body toward the floor.
Go as far as you can maintain a neutral spine — beginners should stop when parallel to the floor.
Use your core to pull yourself back to the starting position. Do not rely on your hips to drive the movement.
This is a demanding exercise — start with partial range of motion and progress gradually.

name: Hanging Leg Raise
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, arms fully extended, body straight.
Brace your core and avoid swinging. Raise your legs by flexing at the hips and, if possible, the knees.
For beginners: raise knees to hip level. For advanced: raise legs until parallel to the floor or higher.
Lower your legs slowly under control — do not let gravity drop them.
If you cannot avoid swinging, use ab straps or a captain's chair as an alternative.
